detail.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: NVIDIA's backlog and purchase commitments have grown faster than its shipment volumes, which is the signature of demand pulling ahead of supply rather than supply chasing weak demand.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex guidance and any broad AI-infrastructure spending slowdown that would let shipments catch up to backlog.
detail.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
detail.title: NVDA demand durability

detail.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM lead times normalize to historical norms while demand indicators stay positive.
detail.mechanism: CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity and HBM memory supply, not wafer starts or end demand, set the ceiling on how many finished GPUs can actually ship in a given quarter.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to announced advanced-packaging and HBM capacity additions from the packaging and memory vendors, which would loosen the binding constraint independent of demand.
detail.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
detail.title: Supply constraint binding

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
detail.mechanism: AMD's Instinct MI-series roadmap and its hyperscaler design-win cadence determine whether it becomes a credible, at-scale second source in datacenter accelerators rather than a niche alternative.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to named hyperscaler deployment announcements and AMD's execution against its MI-series shipment cadence.
detail.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
detail.title: AMD credible second source

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs, or a named ASIC program is cancelled/delayed a generation.
detail.mechanism: Hyperscalers' in-house training and inference accelerator programs compete directly with merchant-GPU purchases for the same capex dollars, so their scale-up can substitute for merchant-GPU demand growth.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex-mix disclosures and the shipment/deployment cadence of named custom-silicon programs.
detail.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
detail.title: Custom ASIC substitution

detail.falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
detail.mechanism: Sustained demand for GPU compute keeps rental and spot prices firm even as new capacity comes online, which indicates the market is not yet oversupplied.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to the D6 rental price track across providers and to the pace at which new GPU capacity enters the rental market.
detail.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
detail.title: Pricing power persistence

detail.falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
detail.mechanism: Export-control restrictions on advanced GPUs limit the addressable market in China and other restricted destinations, redirecting or destroying demand that would otherwise be served.
detail.sensitivity: Sensitive to new export-control rulemaking, licensing decisions, and vendor-reported revenue-impact disclosures tied to restricted destinations.
detail.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
detail.title: Export control exposure

falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Booked orders, financed deployments and multi-GW campus commitments accumulate faster than NVIDIA can ship, sustaining a demand overhang.
rationale: Multiple large fresh design wins and order signals show demand outrunning supply: Anthropic Claude on GB300 in Azure Foundry, Sharon AI deploying up to 40,000 GB300, Firmus 170,000-GPU Indonesia campus, plus NVIDIA's $485B GDP claim and vendors reporting an AI order boom spilling into 2H26 with Vera Rubin demand staying strong through the transition.
sensitivity: Hyperscaler and AI-cloud capex plans and the pace of Vera Rubin supply ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM leadTimes normalize to historical norms for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Advanced-packaging (CoWoS) and HBM capacity gate how much wafer and demand can be converted into shippable accelerators, so realizable revenue tracks packaging/HBM output.
rationale: Supply-side signals stay positive but do not isolate or relieve the packaging/HBM bottleneck: the $500B U.S. buildout explicitly includes packaging partner Amkor, and the Vera Rubin transition finishes 2Q26 with an upswing from 3Q26, implying capacity is still being added rather than lead times normalizing against strong demand.
sensitivity: Packaging (Amkor/TSMC) and HBM capacity additions relative to the Vera Rubin ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
mechanism: AMD ramping Instinct/Helios to mass production creates a viable second source that hyperscalers can adopt to reduce NVIDIA dependence.
rationale: AMD Helios is cited as moving into mass production alongside NVIDIA Vera Rubin, AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU, supporting roadmap execution; however no named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale appears in the findings, so the case for converting the roadmap into material share advances only modestly.
sensitivity: Named hyperscaler adoption commitments and Helios production yield versus NVIDIA availability.

falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named ASIC program is cancelled or delayed by 12+ months.
mechanism: Hyperscalers and large AI labs build custom accelerators for better performance-per-watt and NVIDIA independence, diverting incremental workloads off merchant GPUs.
rationale: Custom-silicon momentum broadens: AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are moving into mass production, OpenAI's Broadcom-built 'Jalapeno' inference chip is cited for better perf-per-watt, and Anthropic is in early talks with Samsung on a 2nm custom chip as part of diversification away from merchant GPUs — though Anthropic simultaneously going GA on GB300 shows displacement of demand growth, not absolute demand.
sensitivity: Custom-silicon perf-per-watt gains versus NVIDIA and whether labs run ASIC and merchant GPUs in parallel rather than substituting.

falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
mechanism: Persistent supply-demand tightness lets GPU-cloud providers hold on-demand rental rates even as new capacity comes online.
rationale: Rental levels hold: Lambda B200 at $6.69/GPU-hr is unchanged from the prior D6 baseline and H100 SXM sits at $3.99, while CoreWeave lists firm per-instance prices (e.g., HGX B200 $68.80, HGX H100 $49.24); no provider shows the ≥15% decline the trigger requires despite ongoing supply additions.
sensitivity: Pace of new rental capacity coming online versus incremental rental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
mechanism: Export licensing restricts NVIDIA sales into controlled regions, shrinking addressable merchant-GPU demand when policy tightens.
rationale: No export-control policy action or vendor-reported impact appears this cycle, and international buildouts proceed unhindered — notably Firmus's 360 MW, up-to-170,000-GPU campus in Batam, Indonesia — signaling addressable merchant-GPU demand is being served abroad; this is one clean cycle, short of the two-quarter threshold to break the thesis.
sensitivity: U.S. licensing policy changes and whether controlled-region demand can be redirected to permitted geographies.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA discloses financing receivables rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a financed AI-cloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed order to cash revenue within a quarter.
detail.mechanism: Vendor financing lowers customers' capital barriers and pulls demand forward, but ties NVIDIA revenue recognition to its own credit extension and the solvency of financed AI-cloud buyers.
detail.sensitivity: Credit conditions and the underlying cash solvency of financed AI-cloud customers.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's own revenue-sharing and credit-support financing increasingly underwrites its accelerator demand, coupling reported demand strength to NVIDIA-extended credit and adding circularity risk to buildout durability.
detail.title: Vendor-financed demand circularity

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA rpoBacklog (multi-year cloud commitments) and its $119B supply commitments grow <10% while Data Center revenue keeps rising, for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Hyperscaler AI capex ($660-690B, heading toward $3-4T) exceeds NVIDIA's packaging/HBM-limited output, so backlog, supply commitments and >12-month lead times accumulate faster than shipments.
rationale: NVIDIA Data Center revenue hit a record $75.2B (+92% YoY) with Q2 guided to $91B assuming zero China, multi-year cloud commitments rose to $30B from $27B, supply commitments reached $119B, and management expects to stay supply-constrained through the entire Rubin generation — demand is outrunning shippable supply more, not less.
sensitivity: Hyperscaler capex trajectory and how much custom-ASIC in-housing diverts incremental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS-S/L leadTimes fall below 26 weeks AND HBM3E returns to available (not sold-out) supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Advanced 2.5D packaging (CoWoS) and HBM stacks are the physical chokepoints for accelerator assembly; with both fully booked, shippable units are capped regardless of wafer starts or order demand.
rationale: CoWoS-S/L is fully booked at 52-78 week lead times, HBM3E is effectively sold out for 2026 with double-digit price gains, TSMC 3nm runs at 100% utilization with demand ~3x supply, and even AMD/Intel cite tight supply — the packaging/HBM bottleneck is more acute and specific than last cycle.
sensitivity: Pace of CoWoS capacity expansion and incremental HBM3E/12-hi supply coming online.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue growth falls below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler MI-series deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct accelerator share stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Hyperscalers seeking supply diversification and lower cost adopt Instinct (Meta 6GW), converting AMD's roadmap into recurring datacenter revenue and share.
rationale: AMD Data Center revenue hit a record $5.8B (+57% YoY) with Q2 guided to $11.2B (+46%), and the Meta 6GW Instinct commitment including a custom MI450 shipping 2H 2026 is a named at-scale hyperscaler win; Instinct is estimated to reach $15B+ and 6-8% accelerator share in 2026 as NVIDIA's share slips toward 75%.
sensitivity: On-schedule MI450 ramp in 2H 2026 and AMD's HBM/CoWoS allocation.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA hyperscale/Data Center revenue growth reaccelerates with no new scaled custom-ASIC deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named ASIC program (Ironwood, Trainium 3, MI450-custom) is cancelled or slips ≥12 months.
mechanism: Hyperscalers with concentrated inference/training workloads build cost-optimized ASICs (Ironwood, Trainium), capturing incremental AI compute that would otherwise be merchant GPUs.
rationale: Substitution is now concrete and large — Anthropic deploys >1M Google Ironwood TPUs for Claude inference, AWS claims Trainium 3 is 50% cheaper, and NVIDIA's own 10-K flags customers building workload-specific ASICs — but NVIDIA hyperscale revenue still grew 115% YoY and datacenter GPU shipments rose 19%, so merchant-GPU growth is not visibly decelerating, capping this at reaffirmed.
sensitivity: Relative $/token cost of ASICs vs GPUs and hyperscaler tolerance of software-ecosystem switching costs.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 B200/H100 median $/GPU-hour declines ≥15% at ≥2 tracked providers within one quarter, or H100 median falls below $2.50/GPU-hr for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Packaging/HBM-constrained rollout keeps rental capacity behind demand, sustaining high utilization and firm clearing prices.
rationale: D6 rental levels are essentially flat versus the prior cycle — B200 holds at ~$5.89-6.99/GPU-hr (aimultiple $6.11, RunPod $5.89, Lambda $6.69-6.99, CoreWeave $68.80/8-GPU node all unchanged) and H100 at ~$2.99-4.29 — showing prices holding up as new supply arrives, with no ≥15% decline.
sensitivity: Pace of new datacenter capacity online versus inference demand, and older-gen (H100/A100) erosion as B200/GB200 ramp.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200/advanced-compute China case-by-case licensing and the 25% Section-232 tariff, AND NVIDIA books >$2B China Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Export licensing and tariffs foreclose China compute demand, but overwhelming supply-limited non-China demand absorbs capacity, so controls trim addressable market without constraining realized revenue.
rationale: Net policy direction is loosening — the AI Diffusion Rule was rescinded and H200/MI325X moved to case-by-case licensing — while the residual constraint is immaterial to realizable revenue: NVIDIA guided $91B and grew 92% excluding China, so supply, not export policy, is the binding limit even though China compute stays foreclosed with a 25% tariff.
sensitivity: US-China policy direction and whether non-China demand stays supply-limited enough to mask any China loss.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q discloses customer financing/notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed AI-cloud/neocloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: If NVIDIA underwrote purchases via credit or revenue-share, reported demand would partly reflect NVIDIA-funded orders, creating a loop exposed to customer default — but observed demand is customer-capex-funded.
rationale: This cycle's findings show demand backed by hyperscaler self-funded capex ($660-690B), record cash revenues, and rising RPO ($27B to $30B) with no disclosed NVIDIA financing receivables; the only adjacent risk is rising customer concentration, so the vendor-financing circularity mechanism finds no primary support.
sensitivity: Disclosure of financing receivables and the financial health of concentrated neocloud/hyperscaler customers.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue (from 54%) for 2 consecutive quarters per 10-Q customerConcentration disclosure.
detail.mechanism: With over half of revenue from three buyers, any one pausing capex or in-housing to ASICs materially dents NVIDIA revenue independent of aggregate AI demand.
detail.sensitivity: Hyperscaler capex cyclicality and in-housing/ASIC decisions by the top-3 accounts.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's data-center revenue is increasingly concentrated in a few direct customers, raising demand-durability risk if any single hyperscaler slows orders or shifts to in-house silicon.
detail.title: Customer concentration risk

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or falls below 15% of Data Center revenue.
detail.mechanism: Selling rack-scale fabric (GB200 NVL72, DGX Rubin) locks customers into NVIDIA's system architecture, raising the cost and complexity of substituting merchant or ASIC compute using non-NVIDIA interconnect.
detail.sensitivity: Adoption of open interconnect standards (UALink/Ultra Ethernet) and ASIC clusters built on non-NVIDIA fabric.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's rack-scale networking is scaling faster than its compute, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus merchant accelerators and custom ASICs.
detail.title: Networking attach as systems moat

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA rpoBacklog and supply commitments grow under 10% while Data Center revenue keeps rising, or leadTimes fall below 26 weeks, for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: NVIDIA's data-center sales jumped and its next-quarter guidance rose while package and memory lead times stretched past a year, so buyers keep ordering more than the company can ship.
rationale: NVIDIA posted record data-center revenue of $75.2B, up 92% year over year and 21% sequentially, guided the next quarter up to $91.0B, supply-chain players report an order boom running into 2H26, and Anthropic's models went into production on GB300 in Azure, all while packaging lead times stretched past a year.
sensitivity: One soft quarter of data-center revenue, or lead times collapsing toward 26 weeks, would move this toward reaffirmed or weakened.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS-S/L leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM3E returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand indicators stay positive.
mechanism: Memory shortages stretched NVIDIA's advanced-package lead times past a year, so packaging and memory, not wafers or orders, decide how much GPU revenue can ship.
rationale: High-bandwidth memory shortages pushed H200 and GB200 NVL72 lead times out to 36-52 weeks even as wafer starts and orders stayed plentiful, confirming that packaging and memory set the ceiling on shippable revenue, though simultaneous next-generation ramps signal future relief.
sensitivity: If the simultaneous next-generation ramps cut lead times below 26 weeks, this weakens quickly.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue growth falls below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler MI-series deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: AMD's data-center revenue grew 57% year over year with named OpenAI and Oracle deployments and a rising next-generation roadmap, so it is winning real accelerator volume rather than only sampling.
rationale: AMD grew data-center revenue to $5.8B, up 57% year over year, guided Q2 to about $11.2B, named OpenAI as a core MI450 compute partner alongside Oracle MI355X and Dell/HPE/Supermicro MI350, and held roughly 5-7% accelerator share, so the Instinct roadmap is turning into real volume rather than samples.
sensitivity: A stall in Instinct share below 6% or loss of the OpenAI ramp would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue (D2) growth stays above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Custom accelerators stayed inside their owners' clouds with no rental market while NVIDIA's data-center revenue reaccelerated and GPUs held about 60% of one large cloud's build, so in-house chips add capacity rather than displace GPU demand.
rationale: Hyperscaler custom chips kept expanding but stayed captive with no rental market, GPUs still made up about 60% of one large cloud's AI build-out, and NVIDIA's data-center revenue reaccelerated to record levels, so in-house silicon is adding capacity rather than taking merchant-GPU growth.
sensitivity: External rental of a custom ASIC at scale, or NVIDIA growth stalling, would re-strengthen it.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 B200/H100 median dollar-per-GPU-hour declines at least 15% at two or more tracked providers within one quarter, or H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Rental rates for H100 and B200 stayed well above floor levels and NVIDIA held a 75% gross margin, so buyers still pay premium prices even as new capacity arrives.
rationale: RunPod listed H100 at $3.29/hr, above the $2.50 floor, Lambda listed flagship B200 at $6.69/hr, and NVIDIA held a 75% non-GAAP gross margin with flat guidance, so rental and vendor prices held up as new capacity arrived.
sensitivity: A sharp rental-price cut across providers as 2H26 supply lands would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200/advanced-compute China case-by-case licensing and the 25% Section-232 tariff, and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Washington shifted H200-class China sales from denial to case-by-case review, so the policy now constrains a smaller share of demand than it did under a full ban.
rationale: Effective January 15 2026 the U.S. moved H200-class sales to China and Macau from outright denial to case-by-case review, loosening the policy so it blocks less of the addressable market than before.
sensitivity: Full license removal plus booked China revenue would break it, while a re-tightening of controls would re-strengthen it.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed AI-cloud customer defaults or fails to convert a financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support program that helps AI clouds buy its systems, so part of reported demand now depends on financing NVIDIA itself provides.
rationale: NVIDIA introduced a revenue-sharing and credit-support program that helps AI clouds buy its infrastructure, which is the exact financing channel the thesis flags, so more reported demand now rests on credit NVIDIA extends.
sensitivity: Disclosed notes-receivable growth or a financed-customer default would strengthen it further, while retirement of the program would weaken it.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per 10-K/10-Q customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: NVIDIA's largest direct customer grew to 22% of revenue and its second to 14%, up from about 11% each a year earlier, so a growing share of sales rides on a few buyers.
rationale: NVIDIA's largest direct customer rose to 22% of revenue and its second to 14%, up from roughly 11% each a year earlier, with the four largest topping half of revenue, confirming that sales lean on a handful of buyers and the concentration is growing.
sensitivity: Broadening of the customer base below a 40% top-three share would weaken this.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching by selling Spectrum-X and Quantum together with Blackwell and GB300, so buyers who adopt its racks find it costlier to mix in rival accelerators.
rationale: ADJUSTED: NVIDIA now leads the data-center Ethernet switch market and sells that networking bundled with its GPUs, deepening a full-system moat that raises switching costs versus standalone accelerators and custom chips. NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching in Q1 2026 by pairing Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, and its NVLink systems cut cost per token, but no finding compares networking growth against compute growth, so the sharper claim that networking scales faster than compute is not supported.
sensitivity: Networking revenue growing slower than compute, or losing the switch-market lead, would weaken it.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue (D2) growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: Each NVIDIA generation sharply lowers the cost of generating a token, so workloads that were too expensive become affordable and total GPU consumption rises.
detail.sensitivity: A generation that fails to lower cost per token, or inference demand rolling over, would undercut it.
detail.statement: NVIDIA's each-generation drop in cost per token pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs and widens the market it can serve.
detail.title: Cheaper inference pulls in demand
